_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Phelps County, MO. Record survival and courthouse history. Phelps County was formed in 1857 from Crawford County. No major courthouse fire — records are relatively well-preserved from county formation. Rolla is the county seat.

previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by the current 1903 building. Records transferred intact.

record_losses.cause: County formation from Crawford County — pre-1857 records filed under Crawford County
records_survived: Crawford County records cover the Phelps County area before 1857
record_losses.description: Phelps County was formed from Crawford County in 1857. Records for the area before that date are Crawford County records — and Crawford County lost many pre-1873 records in its courthouse fire. This creates a double gap: the Phelps County area before 1857 is documented by Crawford County records, many of which burned in 1873.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name} was in what is now Phelps County before 1857, the records were filed under Crawford County — and many of those burned in 1873. The family's paper trail may have a forty-year gap from settlement to 1873.
record_losses.source: Missouri State Archives. Guide to County Records on Microfilm: Phelps County.

deeds.gaps: Pre-1857 deeds are Crawford County records (many lost in 1873 fire).
deeds.repository: Phelps County Recorder of Deeds, Rolla; Missouri State Archives

wills_estates.gaps: Pre-1857 probate records are Crawford County records.
wills_estates.repository: Phelps County Probate Court; Missouri State Archives

marriages.gaps: Pre-1857 marriages filed under Crawford County.
marriages.repository: Phelps County Recorder; Missouri State Archives

court_minutes.completeness: Good — from county formation. Civil War-era sessions were irregular but records survive.
court_minutes.gaps: Pre-1857 court records are Crawford County records.
court_minutes.repository: Missouri State Archives; Phelps County Circuit Court

vital_records.completeness: Moderate — better compliance in Rolla than in the rural hollows. The School of Mines community was more likely to register vital events.
vital_records.repository: Missouri Department of Health and Senior Services; Missouri State Archives

substitute_sources.source_type: Missouri School of Mines (Missouri S&T) records
substitute_sources.description: Student records, faculty records, and institutional archives from the Missouri School of Mines (founded 1870) document families connected to the institution.
substitute_sources.repository: Missouri S&T Archives, Rolla; Curtis Laws Wilson Library
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Valuable for families connected to the mining school — enrollment records, student directories, alumni records.

substitute_sources.source_type: Rolla Herald / Rolla New Era newspapers
substitute_sources.description: Local newspapers in Rolla documented births, deaths, marriages, court proceedings, and community events.
substitute_sources.repository: State Historical Society of Missouri; Phelps County Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Good supplement to courthouse records. Particularly useful for the Civil War era when court records may be incomplete.
substitute_sources.source: State Historical Society of Missouri newspaper holdings.

substitute_sources.description: Population schedules 1860-1940 (first enumerated as Phelps County in 1860). Pre-1860 census for this area is under Crawford County.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential baseline, especially for pre-1857 families who must be tracked through Crawford County census records.

narrative_hooks: Phelps County was carved from Crawford County in 1857, and Crawford County's courthouse burned in 1873 — if {ancestor_name} was in the area before 1857, the records that documented {pronoun_possessive} presence may have burned in a different county's fire.

The Missouri School of Mines kept records of everyone who passed through — if {ancestor_name} attended the school, the archives in Rolla may hold enrollment records, grades, and correspondence that survive nowhere else.
Missouri vital records started in 1883 but were poorly enforced in the hollows — if {ancestor_name} was born in a cabin on Little Piney Creek, the birth may never have been registered.
Rolla's newspapers documented the community from the 1860s — if {ancestor_name} died, married, or appeared in court, the newspaper may hold the notice the courthouse never received.
source_references: Missouri State Archives. Guide to County Records on Microfilm: Phelps County.
Missouri S&T University Archives finding aids.
State Historical Society of Missouri newspaper holdings.
Works Progress Administration. Historical Records Survey: Missouri County Records. Jefferson City, 1937-1942.
